description: OT security sectors covered in this repository — aligned with NACSA Act 854 NCII sectors that contain significant OT/ICS environments. Each sector has distinct OT characteristics, primary standards, and regulatory overlaps.
nacsaAlignment: NACSA Act 854 Schedule defines 11 NCII sectors. Six of these have significant OT environments and are covered in detail here. All six are designated NCII sectors — their operators carry s17-s26 Act 854 obligations.

sectors.name: Energy
sectors.description: Power generation (thermal, hydro, solar, combined cycle), transmission, and distribution. Includes TNB (Tenaga Nasional Berhad) generation, transmission, and distribution assets; Sarawak Energy; independent power producers (IPP). The highest-criticality OT sector in Malaysia — grid collapse affects all other NCII sectors.
keyOtRisks: Substation relay manipulation
EMS/SCADA compromise causing cascading outage
Protection system tampering allowing equipment damage
AMI network as pivot into distribution OT
regulatoryOverlap: Energy Commission Act 2001 (Act 610) — regulates electricity supply and tariffs; cybersecurity obligations complement Act 854
Electricity Supply Act 1990 (Act 447) — governs electricity supply licences; operational continuity obligations
Gas Supply Act 1993 (Act 501) — gas supply licensing and continuity obligations
Renewable Energy Act 2011 (Act 725) — relevant for solar/renewable generation OT assets

sectors.name: Water and Sewerage
sectors.description: Water treatment, pumping, and distribution infrastructure. Key operators: Air Selangor (Pengurusan Air Selangor), Syarikat Air Johor (SAJ), Perbadanan Air Melaka (PAIM), Lembaga Air Perak. OT directly controls chemical dosing, filtration, pumping — sabotage could contaminate public water supply.
otEnvironments: Water treatment plant PLC/SCADA (chemical dosing, filtration)
primaryStandards: AWWA (American Water Works Association) cybersecurity guidance
keyOtRisks: Chemical dosing system manipulation (overdose/underdose causing public health emergency)
Pump failure causing loss of supply
False sensor readings masking contamination
Ransomware causing loss of operational visibility (Oldsmar water plant 2021 reference)
sectors.nacsaNote: Water sector OT attacks can cause mass casualty events (chemical overdose). Any water treatment plant designated as NCII should achieve SL 3 minimum for chemical dosing control zones.
regulatoryOverlap: Water Services Industry Act 2006 (Act 655) — regulates water and sewerage services; operational continuity obligations
Environmental Quality Act 1974 (Act 127) — water quality standards; chemical dosing controls
Safe Drinking Water Act (pending) — proposed legislation strengthening water quality and security requirements

sectors.name: Oil, Gas and Petrochemical
sectors.description: Upstream oil and gas production, midstream pipeline transmission, downstream refining and petrochemical processing. Petronas, Carigali, and their contractors and joint-venture partners. Safety is paramount — the TRITON attack targeted a Saudi Arabian petrochemical plant, representing the most dangerous known OT attack to date.
otEnvironments: Upstream: wellhead control, production separator control, gas compression
Midstream: pipeline SCADA, compressor station control, pig tracking
Downstream: refinery DCS, petrochemical plant DCS
sectors.nacsaCopReference: Covered under energy and critical infrastructure CoPs
keyOtRisks: SIS/ESD compromise enabling runaway reaction or explosion (TRITON-class attack)
Pipeline SCADA manipulation causing over-pressure or product contamination
Fire and gas system compromise masking real emergencies
Ransomware causing uncontrolled production shutdown
regulatoryOverlap: Petroleum Development Act 1974 (Act 144) — vests petroleum resources; Petronas licensing and control
Energy Commission Act 2001 (Act 610) — relevant for downstream gas supply activities
OSHA 1994 (Act 514) — occupational safety and health; SIS security supports OSHA compliance for process safety
IEC 61511 (functional safety standard) — mandates SIS integrity; cybersecurity of SIS must not undermine safety function

sectors.name: Transport
sectors.description: Rail, aviation, port, and road transport infrastructure. OT controls include: Prasarana (LRT/MRT/BRT/Monorail) signalling and train control; KTM Berhad intercity rail; MAHB (Malaysia Airports Holdings) airport systems; Port Klang Authority and Westports vessel traffic management; PLUS Expressways traffic management.
otEnvironments: Rail: train control system (TCS), automatic train operation (ATO), signalling (ETCS/CBTC)
Rail: traction power SCADA, CCTV/access control integration
Aviation: instrument landing systems (ILS), ground movement control, baggage handling
Ports: vessel traffic services (VTS), crane control, gate automation
Roads: traffic management centres (TMC), variable message signs, CCTV
keyOtRisks: Train collision from signalling system compromise
Runway incursion from aviation OT compromise
Vessel traffic service disruption causing port collision
Traffic signal manipulation causing gridlock or accidents
regulatoryOverlap: Land Public Transport Act 2010 (Act 715) — regulates land public transport; operational continuity for rail
Civil Aviation Act 1969 (Act 3) — aviation safety regulations; ILS and ground system security
Ports (Privatisation) Act 1990 (Act 422) — port operator obligations
Road Transport Act 1987 (Act 333) — road traffic management system regulatory framework

sectors.name: Manufacturing
sectors.nacsaSectorNumber: Not explicit NCII sector — subset of Trade, Industry and Economy sector
sectors.nacsaSectorLead: MITI (Ministry of International Trade and Industry)
sectors.description: Advanced manufacturing, semiconductor fabrication, food and beverage production, pharmaceutical manufacturing. Malaysia's semiconductor and electronics manufacturing sector (Penang, Kulim Hi-Tech Park) and food processing are increasingly OT-intensive. Industry 4.0 adoption increases IIoT exposure.
otEnvironments: Semiconductor fab: process control for CVD, etch, lithography, CMP
Electronics assembly: SMT line control, test and measurement automation
Food and beverage: pasteurisation control, packaging line, cold chain monitoring
Pharmaceutical: batch manufacturing control (21 CFR Part 11 compliance)
Automotive assembly: robot control, paint shop environmental control
sectors.nacsaCopReference: No specific NACSA CoP — general IEC 62443 framework applies
keyOtRisks: Production disruption (ransomware — primary risk; EKANS/SNAKE targets manufacturing)
Product quality sabotage (pharmaceutical or food batch manipulation)
Intellectual property theft (semiconductor process recipes)
Safety system compromise in chemical-using manufacturing
regulatoryOverlap: Industrial Coordination Act 1975 (Act 156) — manufacturing licences; relevant for NCII-classified manufacturing
OSHA 1994 (Act 514) — occupational safety; safety-critical process control in chemical manufacturing
FDA 21 CFR Part 11 (US regulation — for pharmaceutical exporters) — computer system validation for GMP-compliant manufacturers
GAMP 5 guidance — computer system validation for pharmaceutical manufacturing OT

sectors.name: Building Automation
sectors.nacsaSectorNumber: Subset of Government and ICT sectors for government buildings
sectors.nacsaSectorLead: JKR (Jabatan Kerja Raya) for government buildings
sectors.description: Building Management Systems (BMS) and Building Automation Systems (BAS) for government buildings, data centres, hospitals, airports, and commercial high-rises. Increasingly connected to corporate IT networks for energy management, making them an often-overlooked OT attack surface.
sectors.nacsaCopReference: No specific NACSA CoP — government buildings may fall under Government sector obligations
keyOtRisks: Data centre cooling failure from BMS compromise (critical IT availability impact)
Hospital HVAC compromise affecting sterile environments
Physical access control bypass via BAS exploit
Fire suppression disabling from BMS attack
regulatoryOverlap: Street, Drainage and Building Act 1974 (Act 133) — building safety and maintenance obligations
Uniform Building By-Laws 1984 — fire safety systems compliance; fire alarm and suppression OT
Malaysian Energy Commission — building energy efficiency and smart building standards
Personal Data Protection Act 2010 (Act 709) — BAS CCTV and access control systems process personal data
